coredata.dc:title: Sodium cellulose sulfate-derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for the detection of tetracycline in white shrimp
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Miao
Zhang, Meng
Zhang, Shutian
Tang, Chuan
Hu, Mengdie
coredata.dc:description: The South American white shrimp, a predominant species in aquaculture, has raised concerns regarding food safety due to the detection of residual tetracycline in both water and shrimp tissues. In this study, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots were synthesized for the first time using sodium cellulose sulfate as the precursor and ethylenediamine as the dopant, resulting in a high fluorescence quantum yield of 33.1 %. The carbon dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy. The average size of the carbon dots was found to be 15.60 ± 2.15 nm, and their excellent photostability and thermal resistance were confirmed through a series of stability tests. The N,S-CDs demonstrated selective recognition of tetracycline, with a strong linear relationship between fluorescence quenching and tetracycline concentration (R² = 0.9967). The limit of detection was as low as 0.219 μM. Fluorescence lifetime analysis revealed that the quenching mechanism was attributed to the inner filter effect. When applied to white shrimp samples, the method showed promising results. Overall, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots offer a sensitive, rapid, and simple approach for the detection of tetracycline in food safety testing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sodium cellulose sulfate
Tetracycline
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped
Carbon dots
Fluorescence detection

Detection of TC in white shrimp

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Dalian University \u201CDiscipline Construction project-Interdisciplinary Project \u201D general project (grant number DLUXK-2023-YB-006 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 21606030 ), the Scientific Research Funding Project of the Education Department of Liaoning Province (grant number LJKZ1178 ), the Dalian High-level Talented Person Innovation Support Plan Project (grant number 2019RQ118 ), Key project at central government level: The ability establishment of sustainable use for valuable Chinese medicine resources( 2060302 ), Liaoning Provincial Science and Technology Joint Program: Technical Breakthrough Project (grant number 2024JH2/102600066 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N,S-CDs. (b) Grain size distribution diagram of N,S-CDs. (c) XRD spectrum of N,S-CDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of N,S-CDs.

(a) The full-scan XPS spectra of N,S-CDs. (b) The high-resolution spectra of O1s. (c) The high-resolution spectra of N1s. (d) The high-resolution spectra of C1s.(e) The high-resolution spectra of S2p.

(a) The UV-vis absorption spectrum, excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of N,S-CDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 328 to 398 nm in 10 nm increments.

(a) The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs after 3 h of continuous heating with 85 °C water bath. (b) The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs after 3 h of continuous irradiation with 365 nm UV lamp.(c) The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs in 0.1 M-1.0 M NaCl solution. (d) Effect of different pH on fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of control group and N,S-CDs mixed with Blank, SDZ, MNZ, SM, PG, TC. (b) Fluorescence response of N,S-CDs in the presence of TC. (c) Relationship between fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of TC. (d) The UV spectrum of TC and the excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs.

(a) The fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs before adding TC. (b) The fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs after adding TC.(c) The UV-visible absorption spectra of the TC before and after the addition of the N,S-CDs.
Comparison of sensing effects of N,S-CDs with other effects.
The detection of TC in shrimp through fluorescence analysis.

head.ce:title: Sodium cellulose sulfate-derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for the detection of tetracycline in white shrimp

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Health, Dalian University, Dalian, 116622 China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Life and Health, Dalian University, Dalian, 116622 China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dalian Institute of Marine Traditional Chinese Medicine, Dalian, 116622 China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDalian Institute of Marine Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Quality Ensurance and Sustainable Use of Dao-di Herbs, Beijing, 100700, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Quality Ensurance and Sustainable Use of Dao-di Herbs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department II of Orthopedics, Affiliated Zhongshan Hospital of Dalian University, Dalian, 116001 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliated Zhongshan Hospital of Dalian University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dDepartment II of Orthopedics, Affiliated Zhongshan Hospital of Dalian University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Co-first authors. Both authors have equally contributed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The South American white shrimp, a predominant species in aquaculture, has raised concerns regarding food safety due to the detection of residual tetracycline in both water and shrimp tissues. In this study, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots were synthesized for the first time using sodium cellulose sulfate as the precursor and ethylenediamine as the dopant, resulting in a high fluorescence quantum yield of 33.1 %. The carbon dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy. The average size of the carbon dots was found to be 15.60 ± 2.15 nm, and their excellent photostability and thermal resistance were confirmed through a series of stability tests. The N,S-CDs demonstrated selective recognition of tetracycline, with a strong linear relationship between fluorescence quenching and tetracycline concentration (R² = 0.9967). The limit of detection was as low as 0.219 μM. Fluorescence lifetime analysis revealed that the quenching mechanism was attributed to the inner filter effect. When applied to white shrimp samples, the method showed promising results. Overall, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots offer a sensitive, rapid, and simple approach for the detection of tetracycline in food safety testing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The prawn farming industry is experiencing rapid growth in the aquaculture sectors of Asia and Latin America. Owing to its rapid growth potential at high stocking densities, the South American white shrimp accounts for nearly 80 % of global mariculture shrimp production (Patel et al., 2020). Antibiotics, particularly tetracycline (TC), are commonly applied to treat and prevent shrimp diseases. However, these substances frequently accumulate in shrimp meat, contributing to the emergence of Vibrio parahaemolyticus (Liu et al., 2024), which presents a significant threat to human food safety (Narayanan et al., 2020). The existing detection methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, and mass spectrometry, despite their high sensitivity, rely too much on expensive equipment, entail high maintenance costs, consume considerable time, and fail to fulfill the demand for rapid screening (Ho et al., 2024; Pérez-Silva et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2019). Facing these challenges, there is an urgent need for an economically favorable detection approach that can rapidly identify tetracycline while possessing both anti-interference capability and detection sensitivity (Wang et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2023; Yang, Yin, et al., 2025; Yang, Zhang, et al., 2025).

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials that have gained significant attention in fluorescence probe research due to their exceptional fluorescence properties, water solubility, and biocompatibility (Atchudan et al., 2018; Kaur et al., 2023; Zhong et al., 2023). More importantly, CDs have emerged as promising candidates in food detection because of their specific recognition ability and excellent rapid response mechanism. Untreated CDs often exhibit low fluorescence quantum yields, restricting their applications (Qi et al., 2019). However, the current doping methods often involve the addition of chemical reagents or metal ions, while neglecting the doping of non-metallic elements inherent in biomass materials (Li et al., 2017; Niu et al., 2017)). If the non-metallic elements in the biomass raw materials themselves are utilized to form self-doping, and combined with artificial doping, it is possible to enhance the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots while maximizing resource utilization (Li et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2015).

Sodium cellulose sulfate (NaCS) is a water-soluble anionic polymer characterized by exceptional chemical stability and biocompatibility (Tang et al., 2022). It is synthesized via the sulfonation of natural plant cellulose. During this process, -SO3 − groups replace the original hydroxyl groups (Normakhamatov et al., 2023). This endows NaCS with antiviral, anticoagulant and antibacterial activities, and provides a natural advantage of self-sulfur doping for the preparation of CDs. Despite its potential as a novel biological functional material, NaCS is rarely utilized as a precursor for CDs synthesis.

In this study, the "self-doped sulfur + nitrogen doping" double doping strategy was used for the first time to prepare a fluorescence probe that can rapidly respond to tetracycline content in Penaeus albinus. This mechanism can form a cooperative recognition mechanism of multiple weak interactions, which can be more specific than a single force, and effectively make up for the weak anti-interference ability of traditional detection methods (Wang et al., 2025). Based on its simple preparation process, excellent optical properties, low cost and environmental friendliness, Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) can be used as an efficient and reliable fluorescent probe, which is widely used in food safety monitoring, especially for the rapid and accurate detection of TC residues. This study provides an important theoretical basis and experimental support for the food safety detection technology based on N,S-CDs.

NaCS with a degree of substitution of 0.6 and a molecular weight of 68 kDa, was obtained from the laboratory of Dalian University. Tetracycline (TC), penicillin (PG), metronidazole (MNZ), streptomycin (SM), sulfadiazine (SDZ) were procured from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. The dialysis bag was acquired from Yikang Technology and Trade Co., Ltd., with the model MD 34-500. The syringe filter membrane was obtained from Cobetter, featuring a pore size of 0.22 μm. Sodium phosphate dibasic and quinine sulfate procured from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Citric acid procured from Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium bicarbonate, sodium chloride and acetonitrile were procured from Comieou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. White shrimp were purchased at local markets.

N,S-CDs were synthesized successfully using a one-pot hydrothermal method with a bottom-up approach. The synthesis began by accurately weighing 0.5 g of NaCS, which was then dissolved completely in 40 ml of ultrapure water. Subsequently, 4 ml of ethylenediamine was added as a dopant to facilitate the synthesis process. The mixture was placed on a magnetic stirrer for 10 minutes to achieve uniform dispersion. Following this, the liquid was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor equipped with a Teflon liner and heated in an oven preheated to 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling the reactor to room temperature, the reactants were filtered through a 0.22 μm pinhole filter into a dialysis bag and dialyzed in the refrigerator at 4 °C for 24 h, with water changes every 6 h. Finally, the liquid after dialysis underwent freeze-drying and sealing, resulting in N,S-CDs powder.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis of N,S-CDs was performed using a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope, enabling high-resolution imaging to investigate their morphology and particle size.
The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of N,S-CDs was analyzed using a Bruker D8 Advance instrument over a 10°-80° range to accurately determine the crystalline structure and phase composition.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was performed with Nicolet iS 20 instrument in the range of 4000-400 cm−1 to study the composition of functional groups on the surface of N,S-CDs.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using the Thermo Fisher Nexsa G2 instrument to verify the FTIR results while exploring the element doping on the surface of the N,S-CDs.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy was performed using a JASCO V-560 spectrophotometer. The 1.5 mg/ml N,S-CDs solution was accurately prepared. The N,S-CDs solution at this concentration was used for a series of subsequent tests. Ultra-pure water was used as the blank control. The UV-visible absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs was measured at 200-600 nm.

The fluorescence spectrum was measured using a JASCO FP-6500 fluorescence spectrometer. As the instrument’s maximum fluorescence intensity is 1000, a slit width of 3 nm × 5 nm was used to fully capture the fluorescence peak. The instrument operated at a scanning speed of 1000 nm/min. The sample was continuously measured throughout the experiment, and excitation and emission spectra were recorded at the optimal excitation wavelength. Since the photoluminescence properties of CDs depend on the excitation wavelength, various excitation wavelengths were selected to explore this feature of N,S-CDs.

The stability of CDs is crucial for their detection as it directly impacts the reliability and consistency of results, thus ensuring accuracy in detection. To evaluate the stability of N,S-CDs fluorescence under varying conditions, fluorescence intensity was measured at high temperatures, under strong UV irradiation, in sodium chloride solutions of different concentrations, and across various pH levels.

Initially, the N,S-CDs solution was placed in an 85 °C water bath and exposed to UV light for 3 h separately. Fluorescence intensity was recorded every 30 minutes. Sodium chloride solutions were prepared with concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 1.0 M, along with standard solutions with a pH range of 3 to 10. Subsequently, 1.5 ml of the N,S-CDs solution was mixed with an equal volume of the standard solution to ensure uniform mixing for fluorescence intensity measurement.

FLQY serves as a critical parameter in the evaluation of CDs' fluorescence intensity. Quinine sulfate, which has known fluorescence quantum yields, was employed as a reference standard. The N,S-CDs solution was configured to ensure an absorbance below 0.05. Subsequently, the fluorescence peak areas at 368 nm for both solutions were determined and used in the calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield using the following formula:
Q S
=
Q R
×
(
I S
/
I R
)
×
(
A R
/
A S
)
×
(
η S
/
η R
)
2

QS represents the fluorescence quantum yield of N,S-CDs, and QR represents the fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate (54 %). IS and IR represent the fluorescence excitation peak areas of N,S-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. AS and AR represent the absorbance of N,S-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. ηS and ηR represent the refractive indices of N,S-CDs and quinine sulfate.

Common aquaculture antibiotics were selected to assess the specificity of N,S-CDs in their recognition. Antibiotic solutions were prepared at a concentration of 9 mM, greatly exceeding the national standard limit of 0.1 µg/ml. A mixture of 1.5 ml N,S-CDs solution (0.15 mg/ml), 0.2 ml antibiotic solution, and 1.5 ml PB buffer was uniformly applied onto a quartz colorimetric plate, followed by fluorescence intensity measurement at 368 nm. For the blank control, 0.2 ml of ultrapure water was used instead of the antibiotic solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of linear regression equation and LOD
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to assess the sensitivity of N,S-CDs to TC detection, we set up various concentrations of TC solutions. The correlation between the quenching efficiency of N,S-CDs and TC concentration was examined and is expressed using the Stern-Volmer formula:
I 0
/ I − 1 =
K q
[ Q ]
Where I0 represents the initial fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs, and I represents the fluorescence intensity after adding TC. Kq stands for the quenching constant, and [Q] stands for the TC concentration.

The detection limit is another crucial aspect of CDs detection for TC. LOD is determined using the following formula:
L O D = 3 σ /
K q

The symbol σ is used to represent the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity in a blank N,S-CDs solution with a sample size of 10. Kq refers to the symbol of the slope of the linear equation.
The fluorescence lifetime is calculated by the following formula:

τ =
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
Where τ represents the average lifetimes of N,S-CDs; τ1 and τ2 represent the fitting fluorescence lifetime, respectively; A1 and A2 represent the respective proportions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in white shrimp
ce:section.ce:para: White shrimp were carefully selected for this study due to their prominent role in China’s shrimp culture industry. The first step involved homogenizing market-purchased shrimp with a specialized juicer to ensure uniform blending. Acetonitrile was then used to precipitate proteins from the homogenized mixture. Ultrasonication for 20 minutes was conducted, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 r/min for 10 minutes to separate components efficiently. The supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm pinhole filter to obtain final samples, labeled Shrimp 1, Shrimp 2, and Shrimp 3. TC solutions at concentrations of 30 μM, 40 μM, and 50 μM were carefully added to the three samples during the recovery experiment. Fluorescence intensity measurements were subsequently performed to calculate recovery rates accurately. To ensure experimental accuracy and reliability throughout each stage of our study protocol, every test procedure was conducted thrice according to rigorous scientific standards.

The micromorphology of N,S-CDs was first examined using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a , N,S-CDs display quasi-spherical shapes, uniform dispersion, and particle sizes below 20 nm. The particle size distribution of 100 N,S-CDs was statistically analyzed. As shown in Fig. 1b, the particle size of N,S-CDs exhibited a central distribution, with an average size of 15.60 ± 2.15 nm. The crystallization degree of N,S-CDs was further investigated.

As shown in Fig. 1c, the XRD pattern revealed a distinct diffraction peak at 20°, corresponding to the 002 crystal plane of carbon, indicating a disordered carbon structure in N,S-CDs (Liu et al., 2019; Mohandoss et al., 2023).

Infrared spectroscopy was used to analyze the surface functional groups of N,S-CDs, confirming the successful doping of nitrogen and sulfur elements. As shown in Fig. 1d, there was an absorption peak at 3430 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of −OH and N−H. These functional groups, through their chemical properties and interactions, played a crucial role in enhancing the water solubility of N,S-CDs (Elshenawy et al., 2023). Characteristic peaks displayed at 2927 cm−1, 1629 cm−1, and 1040 cm−1 corresponded to the vibrational modes of CH, CO, and CO bonds respectively. Peaks observed at 2132 cm−1 and 607 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of SH and CS bonds, respectively. Additionally, peaks at 1480 cm−1 and 1331 cm−1 were indicative of the presence of NO3 − and C−N functionalities. These findings provide compelling evidence for the successful doping of nitrogen and sulfur elements onto the surface of CDs (John et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2015).

Elemental analysis of N,S-CDs was further performed using XPS. Fig. 2a shows the XPS survey spectrum, displaying four peaks at 532.1, 401.1, 286.1, and 168.1 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, and S2p states, respectively. The elemental composition was determined to be 30.94 % oxygen, 11.51 % nitrogen, 52.74 % carbon, and 4.81 % sulfur, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2b, the high-resolution O1s spectra, obtained through a meticulous analysis, revealed two distinct peaks. One peak centered on 532.93 eV corresponded to the CO bond, and the other peak centered on 531.38 eV corresponded to the C=O bond. As shown in Fig. 2c, N−H and CN bond peaks were observed at 401.48 eV and 399.53 eV, respectively. Fig. 2d further shows a broad peak at 287.93 eV associated with the C=O bond, indicating the presence of carbonyl groups. A narrow peak at 286.48 eV corresponds to CO or C−N bonds, suggesting potential functional groups. Furthermore, another narrow peak at 284.88 eV was attributed to the presence of CC or CH bonds within the sample, providing valuable insights into its molecular structure and composition. The high-resolution spectra of S2p in Fig. 2e exhibited two distinct peaks at 169.48 eV and 168.23 eV, which were assigned to the binding energies of the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 orbitals of sulfur atoms involved in −C−S covalent bonding interactions within the material. XPS results corroborate the conclusions drawn from FTIR analysis. Additionally, these results offer compelling support for the effective integration of nitrogen and sulfur elements into carbon sites during the synthesis process.

The ultraviolet, excitation, and emission spectra of N,S-CDs were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 3a , the N,S-CDs solution displayed a distinct absorption peak at 285 nm, likely corresponding to the n→π∗ transition of the C=O bond (Chen et al., 2024; Liu, Liu, & Zhang, 2011; Papadopoulou et al., 2024). After extensive testing, the optimal excitation wavelength for N,S-CDs was determined to be 368 nm, with the best emission wavelength at 447 nm. Under fluorescent light, the solution appears yellowish, while under ultraviolet light, it emits a bright blue fluorescence. Two emission peaks can be observed in the fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs, and this phenomenon may be attributed to the following mechanisms. Firstly, it results from the synergistic effect of the quantum confinement effect and the surface state effect. Due to their tiny size, CDs exhibit the quantum confinement effect, which restricts the movement of electrons and holes, leading to the discretization of energy levels and thus generating fluorescence of specific wavelengths (Amiripour et al., 2024). Meanwhile, nitrogen and sulfur atoms are doped on the surface of CDs, modifying to form unique surface states, and electron transitions on these surface states also emit fluorescence (Zheng et al., 2025). The electron transitions under these two different mechanisms respectively correspond to different wavelengths, contributing to the emergence of two emission peaks.

The study investigates the relationship between various excitation wavelengths and emission peaks. Emission profiles were recorded at different excitation wavelengths, centered around the optimal excitation wavelength of 368 nm with intervals of 10 nm. As shown in Fig. 3b, varying the excitation wavelength from 368 nm to 398 nm resulted in a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity and a continuous shift of the emission peak toward shorter wavelengths. Conversely, decreasing the excitation wavelength from 368 nm to 328 nm caused both fluorescence intensity and the emission peak to shift toward longer wavelengths. This observation is consistent with the excitation-dependent fluorescence mechanism of CDs (Cao et al., 2015; Qi et al., 2019).

After various stability tests were conducted to ensure the robust fluorescence characteristics of N,S-CDs under multifarious conditions, it was observed in Fig. 4a that the fluorescence intensity remained largely unchanged even under continuous high temperature conditions at 85°C. This demonstrates the excellent high-temperature resistance of N,S-CDs, critical for applications in demanding environments. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity decreased only slightly after 3 h of continuous UV lamp irradiation, highlighting the remarkable photobleaching resistance of N,S-CDs. The effect of sodium ion strength on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs was examined using NaCl solutions of varying concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4c, changes in the peak profile color indicate that fluorescence intensity remains stable across varying sodium ion strengths. These results suggest that N,S-CDs maintain robust fluorescence properties in complex chemical environments, highlighting their potential for diverse applications (Ma et al., 2025). The fluorescence characteristics of N,S-CDs were subsequently examined under different pH conditions. As shown in Fig. 4d, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed at pH = 3, followed by a gradual decrease of fluorescence intensity as the pH increased. At pH = 10, the fluorescence intensity reached its lowest point. This can be attributed to the protonation of surface functional groups on N,S-CDs, consistent with the pH-sensitive nature of CDs (Ganguly et al., 2019). To minimize the effect of solution pH on the fluorescence properties of N,S-CDs, subsequent experiments were conducted at pH 3 to ensure optimal fluorescence. The FLQY of N,S-CDs was 33.1 %, which was obtained by comparing with the fluorescence peak area of quinine sulfate(Alam, 2024; Kolekar et al., 2024; Meng et al., 2022). This result demonstrates the potential of N,S-CDs as fluorescent materials and provides a valuable reference for related research.

Several antibiotics commonly used in aquaculture were selected to evaluate the specific recognition ability of N,S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5a , adding SDZ to the N,S-CDs solution increased the fluorescence intensity compared to the control group, likely due to complex formation during the reaction. SM, PG, and MNZ reduced the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs to varying degrees; however, the effect was notably weaker than the quenching observed with TC. When establishing the concentration of these antibiotic solutions, we assumed that if a particular antibiotic solution at this concentration did not completely quench fluorescence, then N,S-CDs were not considered to specifically recognize this antibiotic. It is evident from the figure that there is an approximate line, which clearly indicates that TC has completely quenched fluorescence, suggesting that N,S-CDs can specifically recognize TC.

Following confirmation of N,S-CDs' specific recognition of TC, a detailed study was conducted to examine the correlation between TC concentration and fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 5b, the results clearly indicate a consistent decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing TC concentration. After refining the TC concentration gradient, the decrease in fluorescence intensity within the range of 10–100 μM showed a strong linear relationship with TC concentration. Utilizing rigorous scientific methodology and applying the Stern-Volmer formula, we derived a precise linear equation represented as y = 0.014x+0.2687. As shown in Fig. 5c, this calculation yielded an excellent correlation coefficient (R² = 0.9967), highlighting the accuracy and reliability of the experimental results. N,S-CDs developed in this study exhibit an exceptionally low detection limit (LOD) of 0.219 μM for TC, a value notably lower than those reported by previous researchers for comparable sensing platforms. This superior sensitivity surpasses state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating that the N,S-CDs meet and exceed the rigorous requirements for trace-level TC analysis, aligning with international food safety standards (e.g., EU maximum residue limits) and establishing the platform as a reliable and advanced tool for real-world antibiotic residue monitoring (Table 1).

Further investigation was conducted into the mechanism by which TC quenches the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. Generally, fluorescence quenching mechanisms can be categorized into static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and internal fluorescence quenching (IFE) (Zhang et al., 2024). Dynamic quenching leads to a shortened fluorescence lifetime, while static quenching does not change the lifetime but requires ground-state complexes. In IFE, the lifetime remains unchanged but depends on spectral overlap, and it does not involve molecular interactions. Additionally, there may be an FRET, but usually energy transfer requires spectral overlap between the donor and acceptor, and the lifetime may change. Based on this research, the excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs and the UV absorption spectra of TC were determined. As depicted in Fig. 5d, the UV absorption spectrum of TC partially overlaps with the excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs, indicating potential FRET or IFE processes (Zhu et al., 2019). However, FRET requires a decrease in the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs upon the addition of the quencher (Zhang et al., 2022). Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs was further analyzed before and after the addition of TC.

The fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs was measured using a steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer, and the results were further analyzed through fitting and calculation. As depicted in Fig. 6a , the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs is 7.261 ns without the addition of TC, and 7.6757 ns after its addition. The findings indicate that the fluorescence lifetime shows no notable alteration before and after introducing the quenching agent (Su et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2024). IFE is an optical phenomenon, not a molecular level of energy transfer or complex formation, so the decay dynamics of the excited state are not affected, and the lifetime is naturally unchanged. In addition, as shown in Fig. 6c, UV-visible absorption spectra did not show new peaks before and after the addition of N,S-CDs, indicating that no new substances were formed. It can be inferred that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs by TC is IFE (Qiu et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in white shrimp
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of N,S-CDs for detecting TC content in actual samples was subsequently investigated. The white shrimp samples were processed using the labeling and recovery rate methods, resulting in solutions with concentrations of 30, 40, and 50 μM, respectively. After measuring the change in fluorescence intensity, the linear equation was used for comparison. The recovery rate and detected concentration were calculated. To guarantee the precision and reliability of the findings, each experiment was repeated three times. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rates were 99.83 %, 104.06 %, and 95.52 %, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) were all below 5 %, indicating that N,S-CDs can reliably recognize varying TC concentrations with good sensitivity. In order to ensure the reliability of the experimental results, each experiment was conducted three times.

In this study, N,S-CDs with relatively high fluorescence quantum yield were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method. N,S-CDs were characterized and their elemental composition analyzed using TEM, XRD, FTIR, and XPS, confirming the successful doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms. Additionally, NaCS provides a novel direction and raw material for CDs preparation. From an environmental perspective, NaCS, as a cellulose derivative, offers an eco-friendly approach by utilizing biomass raw materials to prepare CDs for detecting antibiotic residues. Economically, this preparation method is cost-effective, easily accessible, and suitable for large-scale production. Regarding application, actual sample tests yielded significant results, and the excellent specificity and sensitivity of N,S-CDs ensure their reliability. More importantly, when N,S-CDs is used to detect TC in white shrimp, it only needs simple pretreatment, which greatly reduces the time used for detection. It serve as fluorescent agents for detecting trace antibiotics, offering a novel solution for the food detection sector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethical statement - studies in humans and animals
ce:section.ce:para: This article explicitly states that it does not involve any animal experiments or human research. The focus of the study or discussion is on topics that do not require direct experimentation with living subjects. This means that the data and conclusions presented in this article are derived from alternative methods such as literature reviews, theoretical models, computational simulations, or other non-invasive approaches. By excluding animal and human research, the article adheres to ethical guidelines that prioritize the welfare of both animals and humans, ensuring that no harm or distress is caused to living beings in the pursuit of knowledge. Additionally, this approach allows for a broader exploration of the subject matter through diverse and innovative methodologies.

Miao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Conceptualization. Meng Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Shutian Zhang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chuan Tang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Mengdie Hu: Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Dalian University “Discipline Construction project-Interdisciplinary Project” general project (grant number DLUXK-2023-YB-006), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 21606030), the Scientific Research Funding Project of the Education Department of Liaoning Province (grant number LJKZ1178), the Dalian High-level Talented Person Innovation Support Plan Project (grant number 2019RQ118), Key project at central government level: The ability establishment of sustainable use for valuable Chinese medicine resources(2060302), Liaoning Provincial Science and Technology Joint Program: Technical Breakthrough Project (grant number 2024JH2/102600066).
